story:
I insist I am not mad because I remember everything.

Hunger, I say, does not live in the stomach but in the soul, and mine has been starving. At Saint Dymphna Asylum they call that a symptom. Then the city announces a public culinary exhibition—part spectacle, part charity—where even “rehabilitation programs” are invited to compete. They keep me locked in the transport van out front while, upstairs, I know my doctor signs the release forms with trembling hands. They want good press. They want a miracle story. They put me in a white coat, pin a paper badge to my coat, and send two orderlies who pretend they’re only there for the cameras.

The challenge is simple: recreate the flavor of a long-forgotten memory.

To me, it sounds like the world has finally offered a kitchen fit for justice.

Under lights white as bone, I carry my plate to three judges: Calder, Havel, Reed. Their smiles are polished, benevolent, and—once you know what to look for—stained.

They do not recognize me. Not because I wasn’t there, but because that night I was only a girl at the edge of the room, pressed into shadow, hands slick with dishwater, face half-hidden by steam. And men like them never remember the staff they do not bother to see. They looked through me the way powerful men look through staff.

They did not see me the night they killed my sister, Kokki, either.

Kokki was a prodigy at Marrowhall Estate, an apprentice whose food sang. The master favored her and promised she would lead his kitchen. Calder, Havel, and Reed—fading stars with loud laughter and quiet envy—could not bear it.

They invited Kokki to a private dinner to “celebrate” her genius. Wine arrived already poured. Laughter curdled. Accusations followed. Her knees went soft. Her words began to slur. And then there was an “accident”—a fall, a spill, a neck broken on kitchen tiles. The police believed the story because the men wore good suits and the estate smelled expensive.

In the years that followed, Calder published her recipes under his own name. Havel opened a restaurant built on her menu. Reed took her signature dish, Reminiscence à la Marrowhall, and built an empire. They buried Kokki beneath lilacs and dined on her ghost.

I recreate Kokki’s original dish in every visible detail: red wine–glazed quail with skin blistered gold, pear-and-parsnip purée pale as a shroud, lilac petals sugared like frost. She taught me the dish when I was young enough to only watch and memorize, never important enough to touch. The glaze gleams under the lights, simple and perfect—except for my own small addition. The smell rises like a confession.

The judges smile—the same greedy, polished smiles that devoured her brilliance years before—and I watch the taste drag them backward. Recognition blooms like a bruise. Their chewing slows. Their eyes cloud. The memory crawls up their throats.

“What have you done?” Calder rasps, already pale around the lips.

“I followed Kokki’s recipe,” I tell him. “With my own interpretation of the final course.”

The poison—elegant as guilt—rides in the glaze. I had hidden it among the exhibition’s little bottles and garnish vials, where no one noticed one more theatrical touch. I chose it because I believe they drugged Kokki’s wine first, or because that is the only shape the truth has ever made in my mind. Exhibitions like this love drama: smoke, flame, “edible theater.” When Calder’s chair scrapes back and Havel’s hand starts to tremble, the front rows laugh uncertainly, assuming it’s part of the act. The applause swells in that delayed, stupid way crowds have when they don’t yet understand they’re clapping for a death.

Chairs tip. Hands clutch at throats. Reed makes a sound like he’s trying to swallow a memory whole.

I do not run. You do not flee from perfection. The orderlies shout my name, but even they hesitate—waiting for a cue that never comes.

Later, outside, the air smells like rain and lilac, the scent that clung to Kokki’s hair at her burial.

They call me a murderer. I call myself a chef. To me, they differ only in seasoning.

Back at Saint Dymphna, they take my shoelaces and my belt and anything sharp. Still, in my cell, when the lights flicker, the glaze returns to my nose—sweet, red, final. I hum with Kokki’s voice and keep her recipe alive the only way left to me: by remembering it exactly.

Tell them I am not sorry, I write.

Tell them I served the truth.

Kokki left me her recipe. She left it in me.
